text: The “neuroblastoma of the central nervous system” (CNS) has been referenced in the World Health Organization’s (WHO) classification of brain tumors since 1993 [ 2 ]. However, in recent years, thanks to molecular analyses, forkhead box R2 ( FOXR2 ) alterations have become associated with this tumor type and as a result, the WHO classification renamed the neoplasm “CNS neuroblastoma (NB), FOXR2- activated” [ 4 , 5 ]. These alterations are comprised of complex structural rearrangements for which routine testing is not easily implemented [ 4 ]. Consequently, the diagnosis is confirmed using advanced molecular techniques (Next-generation sequencing or DNA-methylation profiling) [ 3 ]. In this context, routine biomarkers for neuropathologists are needed to facilitate a NB-FOXR2 diagnosis. The aims of this report are to present the French national RENOCLIP-LOC network’s experience diagnosing NB-FOXR2 and to propose an alternative diagnostic approach.
summary: FOXR2 alterations are associated with the CNS neuroblastoma entity and prompted the WHO to rename the tumor "CNS neuroblastoma (NB), FOXR2‑activated." These alterations are complex structural rearrangements that are not easily detected by routine testing. Consequently, diagnosis is confirmed using next‑generation sequencing or DNA‑methylation profiling. The report presents the French RENOCLIP‑LOC network’s experience diagnosing NB‑FOXR2 and proposes an alternative diagnostic approach to facilitate routine identification.
